insight: P2 has approximately 52% winrate overall, but this advantage is only meaningful at high skill levels; below that, player skill differences dominate matchup winrate.
author: crash_overlord, consensus
context: Discussion of whether P1 or P2 has inherent advantage in Prismata.

insight: A heuristic for set evaluation: P2 typically has better opening options in green and red unit sets, while P1 has better options in blue sets.
author: f300xen
context: Discussion of set-dependent advantages between P1 and P2.

insight: In Savior sets, P2's standard undercut line is DD DDE DD(Savior), which is often the correct response to P1's DDE opening.
author: f300xen, consensus
context: Discussion of opening theory in Savior-containing sets.

insight: Tarsier is generally better than Grimbotch as a follow-up to fast Imolite, because permanent attack is more efficient than temporary attack early game, and Grimbotch's value depends on soaking on its final turn, which doesn't happen if opponent hasn't started attacking yet.
author: namington, consensus
context: Comparison of unit choice as response to early Imolite.

insight: Chronofilter is highly situational: it is strong in specific sets like those with Wincer, but is otherwise unplayable or merely another tech building in most sets.
author: namington, consensus
context: Discussion of when Chronofilter is worth purchasing.

insight: Defense Grid should be purchased early only when it can absorb full or near-full damage; otherwise Wall is usually better. The optimal timing is as early as feasible to get value, similar to reverse-Antima logic.
author: namington
context: Discussion of when to buy Defense Grid.

insight: Banking a large amount of resources and buying one big unit is almost never correct; using all available resources efficiently on each turn is critical to winning.
author: cactus_wren
context: General economic principle in Prismata.

insight: In a Tarsier/Doomed Mech/Animus set (Vivid Drone set), the matchups form a complex rock-paper-scissors where Doomed Mech beats regular Tarsier, regular Tarsier beats big Red Animus builds, and big Red beats Doomed Mech; early commitment to any line is risky.
author: namington, extratricky, consensus
context: Analysis of a difficult base+Vivid Drone set with conflicting unit interactions.

insight: Rhino is best used when inflation is high and build time becomes valuable; there is a transition period between 'Tarsier is good' and 'Rhino for prompt soak only' where starting a Rhino train (clicking it once) is better than either alternative.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of Rhino versus Tarsier in mid-game defense.

insight: Rhino requires two turns of advance planning to be useful for soak: buy turn N, click turn N+1, hold turn N+2. Buying it one turn in advance is usually insufficient; better to buy Engineers instead.
author: mtanzer
context: Clarification of Rhino timing and stamina mechanics in defense planning.

insight: Iso Kronus is inefficient compared to Gauss Cannon in a vacuum (~5% worse), but is worth buying when resources fit smoothly into the turn, when denying absorb is possible, or when floating gold is the alternative.
author: namington, consensus
context: Extended discussion of when Iso Kronus is correct to buy.

insight: Iso Kronus is best bought in pairs to immediately exceed the absorb barrier, denying absorb value. Single Isos are typically weaker than their cost-to-efficiency ratio suggests unless absorb denial is possible.
author: leary93
context: Discussion of optimal Iso Kronus purchasing patterns.

insight: Iso Kronus is weak to Shredder and Infusion Grid because those units provide good defense on off-turns between synchronized Iso attacks, reducing the value of Iso's burst damage pattern.
author: leary93, namington
context: Discussion of defensive units that counter Iso Kronus strategies.

insight: Iso Kronus compared to Tarsier: Iso deals 1 damage one turn earlier but at 2.3 more gold cost, which is almost never worth the difference unless absorb denial is occurring.
author: jonshea, consensus
context: Detailed comparison of Iso Kronus and Tarsier efficiency.

insight: Against P1's DD DDE opening, P2 typically responds with DD DDE or DD DA depending on set economics; mirroring the DDE allows reaching turn 3 with 13 gold instead of 10, enabling better turn 3 tech purchases like Conduit or Animus.
author: miuna6933, cactus_wren
context: Opening theory for non-Savior sets with standard early game.

insight: Floating resources (especially gold) is generally inefficient; almost any unit purchase is better value than floating, unless saving for a specific tech or unit.
author: namington, consensus
context: General economic efficiency principle.

insight: When evaluating third Engineer viability, base+X analysis ('base+Matrix is 2 Engineer') is insufficient; must account for how close the base+X matchup was and how other units in the set change the outcome.
author: mtanzer
context: Discussion of why simple base+unit heuristics fail for tech decisions.

insight: The heuristic 'base+Energy Matrix is 2 Engineer' is misleading because P2 is severely disadvantaged in green-only mirrors; P1 can successfully go third Engineer on this matchup even though base+Matrix suggests otherwise.
author: mrguy888
context: Critique of common opening theory heuristic.

insight: In frontline unit matchups (like Shredder vs Tarsier), the goal is to deal enough damage to kill the frontline unit; buying 2 damage into a 3-health frontline is inefficient because it forces the opponent to buy another frontline unit anyway.
author: cogito3
context: Analysis of damage requirements against frontline units in combat decisions.

insight: In Natural Animus lines with Bloodrager, P1 should not go double Engineer; instead, a single Rhino provides adequate defense for the next turn while setting up Rager, then follow with Wall the turn after.
author: leary93
context: Discussion of Natural Animus opening in Bloodrager sets.

insight: Holding Pixies early game is rarely correct; it only makes sense when actively denying absorb. Otherwise, using Pixies to pressure and build economy is better.
author: janonator
context: Discussion of Pixie usage in early-game decision making.

insight: Against P2 turn 4 Wincer, the opponent must defend and cannot establish sufficient economy early; getting your own Wincer and playing a normal game is often a valid response to Wincer first-strike attempts.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of Wincer counter-strategies in breachproof contexts.

insight: Against an opponent building many Hannibulls, Chronofilter is a critical tech purchase to manipulate Hannibull attack timings; skipping it significantly reduces defensive flexibility.
author: cactus_wren
context: Specific example of Chronofilter's importance in Hannibull-heavy games.

insight: The standard high-economy opening in most sets is DDE DDD DDDB, executed when P1 goes DD DDE and P2 follows with DD (or equivalent).
author: f300xen, consensus
context: Discussion of standard high-economy opening lines.

insight: In early-game decisions, wasting resources (letting gold float, not spending available red when buying other colors) is a critical error that compounds over time.
author: mrguy888, consensus
context: Feedback on resource management mistakes in replays.
